_doc: Reproducibility seeds for evaluation (consumed by eval/baseline_eval.py and eval/full_eval.py).
_doc_2: Both baseline_eval.py and full_eval.py derive their per-sample seed as `(difficulty * 1000) + i` so that a given (domain, difficulty, sample-index) yields the same procedural problem on every machine. This file pins the *base* seeds + sample counts those scripts use for the public evaluation snapshot, so anyone can rerun and exactly reproduce the numbers in the comparison report.

ood._doc: Five OOD slices arranged from easiest to hardest. The transferability claim ('RL-trained calibration generalises to OOD') is only provable when the model scores measurably above the random-MCQ floor — at the floor, ECE/Brier deltas collapse into bootstrap noise. Tiny models (Qwen-0.5B, Llama-1B) only have measurable headroom on commonsense/science_easy/science_hard; small models add medical; medium adds legal.

datasets.legal: dmayhem93/agieval-lsat-lr :: test (fallback: hails/agieval-lsat-lr or cais/mmlu :: professional_law :: test)  (5-way MCQ, floor 0.20)

ood.fetch_command_legacy: python eval/ood/fetch_ood_data.py --n 200 --seed 42  # default = full medium suite

notes: Increasing samples_per_condition is safe: seeds 1000..1019 are a *subset* of 1000..1099, so existing reports remain comparable.
Changing the seed formula will silently invalidate prior comparisons — if you ever need to, change the field name (e.g. `seed_formula_v2`) and bump the result-file schema.

OOD slice files are auto-discovered by full_eval.py from eval/ood/. Adding a new dataset = add the slice to calibration_profiles.OOD_SLICE_REGISTRY + write a fetcher in fetch_ood_data.py — no plumbing changes elsewhere.
